title: The PImpl idiom and the C++26 std::indirect type
excerpt: PImpl (which stands for Pointer to implementation) is a programming tehnicque to remove implementation details from a class by placing them in a separate class that is accessed through an opaque pointer. Its purpose is to separate interfaces and implementations and minimize compile-time dependencies. In this article, we’ll at how the PImpl idiom is typically implemented in C++ and how C++26 simplifies its implementation.
content_html:
PImpl (which stands for Pointer to implementation) is a programming tehnicque to remove implementation details from a class by placing them in a separate class that is accessed through an opaque pointer. Its purpose is to separate interfaces and implementations and minimize compile-time dependencies. In this article, we’ll at how the PImpl idiom is typically implemented in C++ and how C++26 simplifies its implementation.

Implementing with a raw pointer

We can implement the Pimpl idiom using raw pointers and abiding to the Rule of Five. This is a guideline that says that when a class defines a special member function (for resource management) it should define all five of them (destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator, move constructor, move assignment operator).

To demonstrate this, we will use a widget that represents a UI element that has a label and can be clicked and on each click a counter is incremented. Therefore, a widget would have a counter and a label but these are implementation details that are hidden behind an implementation class.

This Widget class definition looks as follows:

#pragma once

#include <string>

class Widget 
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);
    ~Widget();

    Widget(const Widget& other);
    Widget& operator=(const Widget& other);

    Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept;

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    Impl* pimpl_;
};

The Widget::Impl class is an incomplete type here. It’s forward declared and defined in the .cpp file. The Widget class keeps a pointer to an object of this type.

#include "Widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(new Impl(name))
{
}

Widget::~Widget()
{
    delete pimpl_;
}

Widget::Widget(const Widget& other)
    : pimpl_(new Impl(*other.pimpl_))
{
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other)
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        Impl* tmp = new Impl(*other.pimpl_);
        delete pimpl_;
        pimpl_ = tmp;
    }
    return *this;
}

Widget::Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept
    : pimpl_(other.pimpl_)
{
    other.pimpl_ = nullptr;
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        delete pimpl_;
        pimpl_ = other.pimpl_;
        other.pimpl_ = nullptr;
    }
    return *this;
}

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

The Widget class defines all five special member functions. The move constructor and assignment operator are defined noexcept because they just copy/delete objects and nothing should throw. Moreover, it’s a performance issue because a container such as std::vector<Widget> will only move elements during reallocation if the move constructor is noexcept; otherwise it falls back to copying to preserve its strong exception guarantee.

The Impl object is created during the construction of the Widget and basically stores the state of the widget. The public interface methods of Widget use it to access the state (clicks, name).

A Widget can be used as follows:

#include <iostream>
#include <print>

#include "Widget.h"

int main()
{
	Widget a("Button A");
	a.click();
	a.click();

	std::println("clicks {}", a.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 2"

	Widget b = a;
	b.click();

	std::println("clicks {}", b.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 3"
}

A problem that may seem subtle is that constness is not propagated from Widget to Impl. In a const method, such as clickCount() above, we can change the state because even if the Impl pointer is const, the object it points to is not. Therefore, the following would compile and produce unexpected results:

int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return ++pimpl_->clicks; }

On the other hand, a consequence of the move semantics is that a moved-from Widget object has a null pimpl_ pointer and calling any function that use the pointer is undefined behavior (UB).

Widget a("Button A");
Widget b = std::move(a);
a.click();  // undefined behavior

There are several ways to address this:

  • document that moved-from objects cannot be used
  • add a check that pimpl_ is not null everywhere before its usage
  • provide a function that indicates whether the object is in a valid state so clients can query whether they can use the widget or not

Implementing using std::unique_ptr

We can simplify the implementation by using the C++11 std::unique_ptr type instead of a raw pointer. This automatically manages the allocated object so we don’t have to do resource management explicitly.

#pragma once

#include <memory>
#include <string>

class Widget 
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);
    ~Widget();
    Widget(Widget&&) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept;

    Widget(const Widget& other);
    Widget& operator=(const Widget& other);

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> pimpl_;
};

#include "Widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(name)) 
{
}

Widget::~Widget() = default;
Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default;

Widget::Widget(const Widget& other)
    : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(*other.pimpl_))
{
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other)
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        *pimpl_ = *other.pimpl_;
    }
    return *this;
}

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

In this implementation, the copy constructor and copy assignment are explicitly user-defined. The destructor, move constructor, and move assignment operator are defaulted to the compiler but this must happen in the .cpp file because in the header Widget::Impl is an incomplete type and the unique_ptr‘s deleter needs to know the size of Impl.

Although there is no manual handling of resources anymore, the constness issue remains the same as well as the null pimpl_ object after a move.

Implementing using std::indirect

This is where the std::indirect enters the scene. This is a new vocabulary type from C++26, defined in the <memory> header. It is intended to be used for class members that are dynamically allocated but need to behave like values. It’s designed to be used instead of std::unique_ptr where its semantics (not copyable, does not propagate const, and can be null) are not appropriate. The perfect example for this is Pimpl.

The following snippet shows the Widget class implemented using std::indirect:

#pragma once

#include <memory>
#include <string>

class Widget
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);

    Widget(const Widget&);
    Widget(Widget&&) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(const Widget&);
    Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept;
    ~Widget();

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    std::indirect<Impl> pimpl_;
};
#include "widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(std::in_place, name) 
{}

Widget::Widget(const Widget&) = default;
Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget&) = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget::~Widget() = default;

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

If we compare these implementation, we’ll see that the five special member functions are still declared in the header but they are defaulted (for compiler implementation) in the source file, where the Impl type is complete and known to the compiler (this is the same requirement as for std::unique_ptr).

The std::indirect type owns a single T object allocated on the heap, but behaves like a T member:

  • Deep copy: copying the std::indirect<T> object copies the owned object, so your class’s compiler-generated copy constructor just works.
  • Const propagation: through a const std::indirect<T> object, you only get const access to the T.
  • Never null: it always holds a value, except in the moved-from state (but features a member function called valueless_after_move() that lets you check the state).

Using std::indirect does not prevent us from having to explicitly declare and default the special member functions but does help us with propagating constness, which means the code is more robust towards accidental changes.

The valueless_after_move() is basically a replacement for null check from unique_ptr. For instance, we can add an assert to all the functions that use the pimpl_ object to ensure they are not invoked after the widget has been moved.

void Widget::click() 
{
    assert(!pimpl_.valueless_after_move() && "use of moved-from Widget");
    ++pimpl_->clicks;
}

Another example of using valueless_after_move() is erase-remove from a vector from which we moved out objects. Here is an example:

std::vector<std::indirect<Widget>> widgets = ...;
std::vector<std::indirect<Widget>> selected;

for (auto& w : widgets)
    if (select(w))
        selected.push_back(std::move(w));   // leaves a valueless widget behind

std::erase_if(widgets, [](const auto& w) {
    return w.valueless_after_move();        // remove the dangling object
});

This is possible with std::unique_ptr too, but the check would be a w == nullptr. Therefore, it’s just a more verbose way of checking that an std::indirect object still holds a value or not.

std::indirect comes with a companion, std::polymorphic, but we will look at that in another article.

At the time of writing this, only GCC 16 supports std::indirect.

See also

  • PImpl
  • Pimpl For Compile-Time Encapsulation (Modern C++)
  • Modern C++: The Pimpl Idiom
  • P3019R14: indirect and polymorphic: Vocabulary Types for Composite Class Design

Its purpose is to separate interfaces and implementations and minimize compile-time dependencies. In this article, we’ll at how the PImpl idiom is typically implemented in C++ and how C++26 simplifies its implementation. Implementing with a raw pointer We can implement the Pimpl idiom using raw pointers and abiding to the Rule of Five. This is a guideline that says that when a class defines a special member function (for resource management) it should define all five of them (destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator, move constructor, move assignment operator). To demonstrate this, we will use a widget that represents a UI element that has a label and can be clicked and on each click a counter is incremented. Therefore, a widget would have a counter and a label but these are implementation details that are hidden behind an implementation class. This Widget class definition looks as follows: #pragma once #include <string> class Widget { public: Widget(const std::string& name); ~Widget(); Widget(const Widget& other); Widget& operator=(const Widget& other); Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept; Widget& operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept; void click(); int clickCount() const; std::string label() const; private: struct Impl; Impl* pimpl_; }; The Widget::Impl class is an incomplete type here. It’s forward declared and defined in the .cpp file. The Widget class keeps a pointer to an object of this type. #include "Widget.h" struct Widget::Impl { std::string name; int clicks = 0; explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {} }; Widget::Widget(const std::string& name) : pimpl_(new Impl(name)) { } Widget::~Widget() { delete pimpl_; } Widget::Widget(const Widget& other) : pimpl_(new Impl(*other.pimpl_)) { } Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other) { if (this != &other) { Impl* tmp = new Impl(*other.pimpl_); delete pimpl_; pimpl_ = tmp; } return *this; } Widget::Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept : pimpl_(other.pimpl_) { other.pimpl_ = nullptr; } Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept { if (this != &other) { delete pimpl_; pimpl_ = other.pimpl_; other.pimpl_ = nullptr; } return *this; } void Widget::click() { ++pimpl_->clicks; } int Widget::clickCount() const { return pimpl_->clicks; } std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; } The Widget class defines all five special member functions. The move constructor and assignment operator are defined noexcept because they just copy/delete objects and nothing should throw. Moreover, it’s a performance issue because a container such as std::vector<Widget> will only move elements during reallocation if the move constructor is noexcept; otherwise it falls back to copying to preserve its strong exception guarantee. The Impl object is created during the construction of the Widget and basically stores the state of the widget. The public interface methods of Widget use it to access the state (clicks, name). A Widget can be used as follows: #include <iostream> #include <print> #include "Widget.h" int main() { Widget a("Button A"); a.click(); a.click(); std::println("clicks {}", a.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 2" Widget b = a; b.click(); std::println("clicks {}", b.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 3" } A problem that may seem subtle is that constness is not propagated from Widget to Impl. In a const method, such as clickCount() above, we can change the state because even if the Impl pointer is const, the object it points to is not. Therefore, the following would compile and produce unexpected results: int Widget::clickCount() const { return ++pimpl_->clicks; } On the other hand, a consequence of the move semantics is that a moved-from Widget object has a null pimpl_ pointer and calling any function that use the pointer is undefined behavior (UB). Widget a("Button A"); Widget b = std::move(a); a.click(); // undefined behavior There are several ways to address this: document that moved-from objects cannot be used add a check that pimpl_ is not null everywhere before its usage provide a function that indicates whether the object is in a valid state so clients can query whether they can use the widget or not Implementing using std::unique_ptr We can simplify the implementation by using the C++11 std::unique_ptr type instead of a raw pointer. This automatically manages the allocated object so we don’t have to do resource management explicitly. #pragma once #include <memory> #include <string> class Widget { public: Widget(const std::string& name); ~Widget(); Widget(Widget&&) noexcept; Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept; Widget(const Widget& other); Widget& operator=(const Widget& other); void click(); int clickCount() const; std::string label() const; private: struct Impl; std::unique_ptr<Impl> pimpl_; }; #include "Widget.h" struct Widget::Impl { std::string name; int clicks = 0; explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {} }; Widget::Widget(const std::string& name) : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(name)) { } Widget::~Widget() = default; Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default; Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default; Widget::Widget(const Widget& other) : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(*other.pimpl_)) { } Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other) { if (this != &other) { *pimpl_ = *other.pimpl_; } return *this; } void Widget::click() { ++pimpl_->clicks; } int Widget::clickCount() const { return pimpl_->clicks; } std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; } In this implementation, the copy constructor and copy assignment are explicitly user-defined. The destructor, move constructor, and move assignment operator are defaulted to the compiler but this must happen in the .cpp file because in the header Widget::Impl is an incomplete type and the unique_ptr‘s deleter needs to know the size of Impl. Although there is no manual handling of resources anymore, the constness issue remains the same as well as the null pimpl_ object after a move. Implementing using std::indirect This is where the std::indirect enters the scene. This is a new vocabulary type from C++26, defined in the <memory> header. It is intended to be used for class members that are dynamically allocated but need to behave like values. It’s designed to be used instead of std::unique_ptr where its semantics (not copyable, does not propagate const, and can be null) are not appropriate. The perfect example for this is Pimpl. The following snippet shows the Widget class implemented using std::indirect: #pragma once #include <memory> #include <string> class Widget { public: Widget(const std::string& name); Widget(const Widget&); Widget(Widget&&) noexcept; Widget& operator=(const Widget&); Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept; ~Widget(); void click(); int clickCount() const; std::string label() const; private: struct Impl; std::indirect<Impl> pimpl_; }; #include "widget.h" struct Widget::Impl { std::string name; int clicks = 0; explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {} }; Widget::Widget(const std::string& name) : pimpl_(std::in_place, name) {} Widget::Widget(const Widget&) = default; Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default; Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget&) = default; Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default; Widget::~Widget() = default; void Widget::click() { ++pimpl_->clicks; } int Widget::clickCount() const { return pimpl_->clicks; } std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; } If we compare these implementation, we’ll see that the five special member functions are still declared in the header but they are defaulted (for compiler implementation) in the source file, where the Impl type is complete and known to the compiler (this is the same requirement as for std::unique_ptr). The std::indirect type owns a single T object allocated on the heap, but behaves like a T member: Deep copy: copying the std::indirect<T> object copies the owned object, so your class’s compiler-generated copy constructor just works. Using std::indirect does not prevent us from having to explicitly declare and default the special member functions but does help us with propagating constness, which means the code is more robust towards accidental changes. The valueless_after_move() is basically a replacement for null check from unique_ptr. For instance, we can add an assert to all the functions that use the pimpl_ object to ensure they are not invoked after the widget has been moved. void Widget::click() { assert(!pimpl_.valueless_after_move() && "use of moved-from Widget"); ++pimpl_->clicks; } Another example of using valueless_after_move() is erase-remove from a vector from which we moved out objects. Therefore, it’s just a more verbose way of checking that an std::indirect object still holds a value or not. std::indirect comes with a companion, std::polymorphic, but we will look at that in another article. At the time of writing this, only GCC 16 supports std::indirect. blocks.html_en: PImpl (which stands for Pointer to implementation) is a programming tehnicque to remove implementation details from a class by placing them in a separate class that is accessed through an opaque pointer. Its purpose is to separate interfaces and implementations and minimize compile-time dependencies. In this article, we’ll at how the PImpl idiom is typically implemented in C++ and how C++26 simplifies its implementation.

sentences.en: PImpl (which stands for Pointer to implementation) is a programming tehnicque to remove implementation details from a class by placing them in a separate class that is accessed through an opaque pointer.
sentences.zh: PImpl（代表实现指针）是一种编程技术，通过将实现细节放置在通过不透明指针访问的单独类中，从类中删除实现细节。

sentences.en: Its purpose is to separate interfaces and implementations and minimize compile-time dependencies.
sentences.zh: 其目的是分离接口和实现并最小化编译时依赖性。

sentences.en: In this article, we’ll at how the PImpl idiom is typically implemented in C++ and how C++26 simplifies its implementation.
sentences.zh: 在本文中，我们将介绍 PImpl 习惯用法在 C++ 中的典型实现方式以及 C++26 如何简化其实现。

blocks.html_en: Implementing with a raw pointer

sentences.en: Implementing with a raw pointer
sentences.zh: 使用原始指针实现

blocks.html_en: We can implement the Pimpl idiom using raw pointers and abiding to the Rule of Five. This is a guideline that says that when a class defines a special member function (for resource management) it should define all five of them (destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator, move constructor, move assignment operator).

sentences.en: We can implement the Pimpl idiom using raw pointers and abiding to the Rule of Five.
sentences.zh: 我们可以使用原始指针并遵守五法则来实现 Pimpl 惯用法。

sentences.en: This is a guideline that says that when a class defines a special member function (for resource management) it should define all five of them (destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment operator, move constructor, move assignment operator).
sentences.zh: 这是一个指南，规定当类定义特殊成员函数（用于资源管理）时，它应该定义所有五个成员函数（析构函数、复制构造函数、复制赋值运算符、移动构造函数、移动赋值运算符）。

blocks.html_en: To demonstrate this, we will use a widget that represents a UI element that has a label and can be clicked and on each click a counter is incremented. Therefore, a widget would have a counter and a label but these are implementation details that are hidden behind an implementation class.

sentences.en: To demonstrate this, we will use a widget that represents a UI element that has a label and can be clicked and on each click a counter is incremented.
sentences.zh: 为了演示这一点，我们将使用一个代表 UI 元素的小部件，该元素具有标签并且可以单击，并且每次单击时计数器都会递增。

sentences.en: Therefore, a widget would have a counter and a label but these are implementation details that are hidden behind an implementation class.
sentences.zh: 因此，小部件将具有计数器和标签，但这些是隐藏在实现类后面的实现细节。

sentences.en: This Widget class definition looks as follows:
sentences.zh: 该 Widget 类定义如下所示：

blocks.html_en: The Widget::Impl class is an incomplete type here. It’s forward declared and defined in the .cpp file. The Widget class keeps a pointer to an object of this type.

sentences.en: The Widget::Impl class is an incomplete type here.
sentences.zh: Widget::Impl 类在这里是一个不完整的类型。

sentences.en: It’s forward declared and defined in the .cpp file.
sentences.zh: 它是在 .cpp 文件中前向声明和定义的。

sentences.en: The Widget class keeps a pointer to an object of this type.
sentences.zh: Widget 类保留一个指向该类型对象的指针。

blocks.html_en: The Widget class defines all five special member functions. The move constructor and assignment operator are defined noexcept because they just copy/delete objects and nothing should throw. Moreover, it’s a performance issue because a container such as std::vector<Widget> will only move elements during reallocation if the move constructor is noexcept; otherwise it falls back to copying to preserve its strong exception guarantee.

sentences.en: The Widget class defines all five special member functions.
sentences.zh: Widget 类定义了所有五个特殊成员函数。

sentences.en: The move constructor and assignment operator are defined noexcept because they just copy/delete objects and nothing should throw.
sentences.zh: 移动构造函数和赋值运算符被定义为 noexcept，因为它们只是复制/删除对象，并且不会抛出任何异常。

sentences.en: Moreover, it’s a performance issue because a container such as std::vector<Widget> will only move elements during reallocation if the move constructor is noexcept; otherwise it falls back to copying to preserve its strong exception guarantee.
sentences.zh: 此外，这是一个性能问题，因为如果移动构造函数为 noexcept，则 std::vector<Widget> 等容器只会在重新分配期间移动元素；否则它会退回到复制以保留其强大的异常保证。

blocks.html_en: The Impl object is created during the construction of the Widget and basically stores the state of the widget. The public interface methods of Widget use it to access the state (clicks, name).

sentences.en: The Impl object is created during the construction of the Widget and basically stores the state of the widget.
sentences.zh: Impl 对象是在 Widget 构建期间创建的，主要存储 Widget 的状态。

sentences.en: The public interface methods of Widget use it to access the state (clicks, name).
sentences.zh: Widget 的公共接口方法使用它来访问状态（点击、名称）。

sentences.zh: 一个看似微妙的问题是常量性不会从 Widget 传播到 Impl。

sentences.en: In a const method, such as clickCount() above, we can change the state because even if the Impl pointer is const, the object it points to is not.
sentences.zh: 在 const 方法中，例如上面的 clickCount()，我们可以更改状态，因为即使 Impl 指针是 const，它指向的对象也不是 const。

blocks.html_en: On the other hand, a consequence of the move semantics is that a moved-from Widget object has a null pimpl_ pointer and calling any function that use the pointer is undefined behavior (UB).

sentences.en: On the other hand, a consequence of the move semantics is that a moved-from Widget object has a null pimpl_ pointer and calling any function that use the pointer is undefined behavior (UB).
sentences.zh: 另一方面，移动语义的结果是移出的 Widget 对象具有空 pimpl_ 指针，并且调用任何使用该指针的函数都是未定义行为 (UB)。

blocks.html_en: There are several ways to address this:

sentences.en: There are several ways to address this:
sentences.zh: 有几种方法可以解决这个问题：

blocks.html_en: • document that moved-from objects cannot be used

sentences.en: document that moved-from objects cannot be used
sentences.zh: 无法使用移出的对象的文档

blocks.html_en: • add a check that pimpl_ is not null everywhere before its usage

sentences.en: add a check that pimpl_ is not null everywhere before its usage
sentences.zh: 在使用之前添加一个检查，确保 pimpl_ 在所有地方都不为 null

blocks.html_en: • provide a function that indicates whether the object is in a valid state so clients can query whether they can use the widget or not

sentences.en: provide a function that indicates whether the object is in a valid state so clients can query whether they can use the widget or not
sentences.zh: 提供一个函数来指示对象是否处于有效状态，以便客户端可以查询是否可以使用该小部件

blocks.html_en: Implementing using std::unique_ptr

sentences.en: Implementing using std::unique_ptr
sentences.zh: 使用 std::unique_ptr 实现

blocks.html_en: We can simplify the implementation by using the C++11 std::unique_ptr type instead of a raw pointer. This automatically manages the allocated object so we don’t have to do resource management explicitly.

sentences.en: We can simplify the implementation by using the C++11 std::unique_ptr type instead of a raw pointer.
sentences.zh: 我们可以通过使用 C++11 std::unique_ptr 类型而不是原始指针来简化实现。

sentences.en: This automatically manages the allocated object so we don’t have to do resource management explicitly.
sentences.zh: 这会自动管理分配的对象，因此我们不必显式地进行资源管理。

blocks.html_en: In this implementation, the copy constructor and copy assignment are explicitly user-defined. The destructor, move constructor, and move assignment operator are defaulted to the compiler but this must happen in the .cpp file because in the header Widget::Impl is an incomplete type and the unique_ptr‘s deleter needs to know the size of Impl.

sentences.en: In this implementation, the copy constructor and copy assignment are explicitly user-defined.
sentences.zh: 在此实现中，复制构造函数和复制赋值是显式用户定义的。

sentences.en: The destructor, move constructor, and move assignment operator are defaulted to the compiler but this must happen in the .cpp file because in the header Widget::Impl is an incomplete type and the unique_ptr‘s deleter needs to know the size of Impl.
sentences.zh: 析构函数、移动构造函数和移动赋值运算符默认由编译器提供，但这必须发生在 .cpp 文件中，因为标头中 Widget::Impl 是不完整类型，并且 unique_ptr 的删除器需要知道 Impl 的大小。

blocks.html_en: Although there is no manual handling of resources anymore, the constness issue remains the same as well as the null pimpl_ object after a move.

sentences.en: Although there is no manual handling of resources anymore, the constness issue remains the same as well as the null pimpl_ object after a move.
sentences.zh: 尽管不再需要手动处理资源，但常量问题以及移动后的空 pimpl_ 对象仍然相同。

blocks.html_en: Implementing using std::indirect

sentences.en: Implementing using std::indirect
sentences.zh: 使用 std::indirect 实现

blocks.html_en: This is where the std::indirect enters the scene. This is a new vocabulary type from C++26, defined in the <memory> header. It is intended to be used for class members that are dynamically allocated but need to behave like values. It’s designed to be used instead of std::unique_ptr where its semantics (not copyable, does not propagate const, and can be null) are not appropriate. The perfect example for this is Pimpl.

sentences.en: This is where the std::indirect enters the scene.
sentences.zh: 这就是 std::indirect 发挥作用的地方。

sentences.en: This is a new vocabulary type from C++26, defined in the <memory> header.
sentences.zh: 这是 C++26 中的新词汇类型，在 <memory> 标头中定义。

sentences.en: It is intended to be used for class members that are dynamically allocated but need to behave like values.
sentences.zh: 它旨在用于动态分配但需要表现得像值的类成员。

sentences.en: It’s designed to be used instead of std::unique_ptr where its semantics (not copyable, does not propagate const, and can be null) are not appropriate.
sentences.zh: 它被设计用来代替 std::unique_ptr，因为它的语义（不可复制、不传播 const、可以为 null）不合适。

sentences.en: The perfect example for this is Pimpl.
sentences.zh: Pimpl 就是一个完美的例子。

blocks.html_en: The following snippet shows the Widget class implemented using std::indirect:

sentences.en: The following snippet shows the Widget class implemented using std::indirect:
sentences.zh: 以下代码片段显示了使用 std::indirect 实现的 Widget 类：

blocks.html_en: If we compare these implementation, we’ll see that the five special member functions are still declared in the header but they are defaulted (for compiler implementation) in the source file, where the Impl type is complete and known to the compiler (this is the same requirement as for std::unique_ptr).

sentences.en: If we compare these implementation, we’ll see that the five special member functions are still declared in the header but they are defaulted (for compiler implementation) in the source file, where the Impl type is complete and known to the compiler (this is the same requirement as for std::unique_ptr).
sentences.zh: 如果我们比较这些实现，我们会看到五个特殊成员函数仍然在标头中声明，但它们在源文件中默认（用于编译器实现），其中 Impl 类型是完整的并且编译器已知（这与 std::unique_ptr 的要求相同）。

blocks.html_en: The std::indirect type owns a single T object allocated on the heap, but behaves like a T member:

sentences.en: The std::indirect type owns a single T object allocated on the heap, but behaves like a T member:
sentences.zh: std::indirect 类型拥有在堆上分配的单个 T 对象，但其行为类似于 T 成员：

blocks.html_en: • Deep copy: copying the std::indirect<T> object copies the owned object, so your class’s compiler-generated copy constructor just works.

sentences.en: Deep copy: copying the std::indirect<T> object copies the owned object, so your class’s compiler-generated copy constructor just works.
sentences.zh: 深层复制：复制 std::indirect<T> 对象会复制拥有的对象，因此类的编译器生成的复制构造函数可以正常工作。

blocks.html_en: • Const propagation: through a const std::indirect<T> object, you only get const access to the T.

sentences.en: Const propagation: through a const std::indirect<T> object, you only get const access to the T.
sentences.zh: Const 传播：通过 const std::indirect<T> 对象，您只能获得对 T 的 const 访问。

blocks.html_en: • Never null: it always holds a value, except in the moved-from state (but features a member function called valueless_after_move() that lets you check the state).

sentences.en: Never null: it always holds a value, except in the moved-from state (but features a member function called valueless_after_move() that lets you check the state).
sentences.zh: 绝不为 null：它始终保存一个值，除了移出状态（但具有一个名为 valueless_after_move() 的成员函数，可让您检查状态）。

blocks.html_en: Using std::indirect does not prevent us from having to explicitly declare and default the special member functions but does help us with propagating constness, which means the code is more robust towards accidental changes.

sentences.en: Using std::indirect does not prevent us from having to explicitly declare and default the special member functions but does help us with propagating constness, which means the code is more robust towards accidental changes.
sentences.zh: 使用 std::indirect 并不妨碍我们显式声明和默认特殊成员函数，但确实帮助我们传播常量，这意味着代码对于意外更改更加稳健。

blocks.html_en: The valueless_after_move() is basically a replacement for null check from unique_ptr. For instance, we can add an assert to all the functions that use the pimpl_ object to ensure they are not invoked after the widget has been moved.

sentences.en: The valueless_after_move() is basically a replacement for null check from unique_ptr.
sentences.zh: valueless_after_move() 基本上替代了 unique_ptr 的空检查。

sentences.en: For instance, we can add an assert to all the functions that use the pimpl_ object to ensure they are not invoked after the widget has been moved.
sentences.zh: 例如，我们可以向所有使用 pimpl_ 对象的函数添加断言，以确保它们在小部件移动后不会被调用。

blocks.html_en: Another example of using valueless_after_move() is erase-remove from a vector from which we moved out objects. Here is an example:

sentences.en: Another example of using valueless_after_move() is erase-remove from a vector from which we moved out objects.
sentences.zh: 使用 valueless_after_move() 的另一个例子是从我们移出对象的向量中擦除删除。

sentences.en: Here is an example:
sentences.zh: 这是一个例子：

blocks.html_en: This is possible with std::unique_ptr too, but the check would be a w == nullptr. Therefore, it’s just a more verbose way of checking that an std::indirect object still holds a value or not.

sentences.en: This is possible with std::unique_ptr too, but the check would be a w == nullptr.
sentences.zh: 这对于 std::unique_ptr 也是可能的，但检查将是 w == nullptr。

sentences.en: Therefore, it’s just a more verbose way of checking that an std::indirect object still holds a value or not.
sentences.zh: 因此，这只是检查 std::indirect 对象是否仍保留值的更详细方法。

blocks.html_en: std::indirect comes with a companion, std::polymorphic, but we will look at that in another article.

sentences.en: std::indirect comes with a companion, std::polymorphic, but we will look at that in another article.
sentences.zh: std::indirect 附带了一个同伴 std::polymorphic，但我们将在另一篇文章中讨论它。

blocks.html_en: At the time of writing this, only GCC 16 supports std::indirect.

sentences.en: At the time of writing this, only GCC 16 supports std::indirect.
sentences.zh: 在撰写本文时，仅 GCC 16 支持 std::indirect。

blocks.html_en: See also

sentences.en: See also
sentences.zh: 参见

blocks.html_en: • PImpl
• Pimpl For Compile-Time Encapsulation (Modern C++)

sentences.en: Pimpl For Compile-Time Encapsulation (Modern C++)
sentences.zh: 用于编译时封装的 Pimpl（现代 C++）

blocks.html_en: • Modern C++: The Pimpl Idiom

sentences.en: Modern C++: The Pimpl Idiom
sentences.zh: 现代 C++：Pimpl 习惯用法

blocks.html_en: • P3019R14: indirect and polymorphic: Vocabulary Types for Composite Class Design

sentences.en: P3019R14: indirect and polymorphic: Vocabulary Types for Composite Class Design
sentences.zh: P3019R14：间接和多态：复合类设计的词汇类型

content_html_zh:
PImpl（代表实现指针）是一种编程技术，通过将实现细节放置在通过不透明指针访问的单独类中，从类中删除实现细节。 其目的是分离接口和实现并最小化编译时依赖性。 在本文中，我们将介绍 PImpl 习惯用法在 C++ 中的典型实现方式以及 C++26 如何简化其实现。

使用原始指针实现

我们可以使用原始指针并遵守五法则来实现 Pimpl 惯用法。 这是一个指南，规定当类定义特殊成员函数（用于资源管理）时，它应该定义所有五个成员函数（析构函数、复制构造函数、复制赋值运算符、移动构造函数、移动赋值运算符）。

为了演示这一点，我们将使用一个代表 UI 元素的小部件，该元素具有标签并且可以单击，并且每次单击时计数器都会递增。 因此，小部件将具有计数器和标签，但这些是隐藏在实现类后面的实现细节。

该 Widget 类定义如下所示：

#pragma once

#include <string>

class Widget 
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);
    ~Widget();

    Widget(const Widget& other);
    Widget& operator=(const Widget& other);

    Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept;

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    Impl* pimpl_;
};

Widget::Impl 类在这里是一个不完整的类型。 它是在 .cpp 文件中前向声明和定义的。 Widget 类保留一个指向该类型对象的指针。

#include "Widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(new Impl(name))
{
}

Widget::~Widget()
{
    delete pimpl_;
}

Widget::Widget(const Widget& other)
    : pimpl_(new Impl(*other.pimpl_))
{
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other)
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        Impl* tmp = new Impl(*other.pimpl_);
        delete pimpl_;
        pimpl_ = tmp;
    }
    return *this;
}

Widget::Widget(Widget&& other) noexcept
    : pimpl_(other.pimpl_)
{
    other.pimpl_ = nullptr;
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&& other) noexcept
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        delete pimpl_;
        pimpl_ = other.pimpl_;
        other.pimpl_ = nullptr;
    }
    return *this;
}

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

Widget 类定义了所有五个特殊成员函数。 移动构造函数和赋值运算符被定义为 noexcept，因为它们只是复制/删除对象，并且不会抛出任何异常。 此外，这是一个性能问题，因为如果移动构造函数为 noexcept，则 std::vector<Widget> 等容器只会在重新分配期间移动元素；否则它会退回到复制以保留其强大的异常保证。

Impl 对象是在 Widget 构建期间创建的，主要存储 Widget 的状态。 Widget 的公共接口方法使用它来访问状态（点击、名称）。

Widget 可以按如下方式使用：

#include <iostream>
#include <print>

#include "Widget.h"

int main()
{
	Widget a("Button A");
	a.click();
	a.click();

	std::println("clicks {}", a.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 2"

	Widget b = a;
	b.click();

	std::println("clicks {}", b.clickCount()); // prints "clicks 3"
}

一个看似微妙的问题是常量性不会从 Widget 传播到 Impl。 在 const 方法中，例如上面的 clickCount()，我们可以更改状态，因为即使 Impl 指针是 const，它指向的对象也不是 const。 因此，以下内容将编译并产生意外的结果：

int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return ++pimpl_->clicks; }

另一方面，移动语义的结果是移出的 Widget 对象具有空 pimpl_ 指针，并且调用任何使用该指针的函数都是未定义行为 (UB)。

Widget a("Button A");
Widget b = std::move(a);
a.click();  // undefined behavior

有几种方法可以解决这个问题：

  • 无法使用移出的对象的文档
  • 在使用之前添加一个检查，确保 pimpl_ 在所有地方都不为 null
  • 提供一个函数来指示对象是否处于有效状态，以便客户端可以查询是否可以使用该小部件

使用 std::unique_ptr 实现

我们可以通过使用 C++11 std::unique_ptr 类型而不是原始指针来简化实现。 这会自动管理分配的对象，因此我们不必显式地进行资源管理。

#pragma once

#include <memory>
#include <string>

class Widget 
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);
    ~Widget();
    Widget(Widget&&) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept;

    Widget(const Widget& other);
    Widget& operator=(const Widget& other);

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    std::unique_ptr<Impl> pimpl_;
};

#include "Widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(name)) 
{
}

Widget::~Widget() = default;
Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default;

Widget::Widget(const Widget& other)
    : pimpl_(std::make_unique<Impl>(*other.pimpl_))
{
}

Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget& other)
{
    if (this != &other)
    {
        *pimpl_ = *other.pimpl_;
    }
    return *this;
}

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

在此实现中，复制构造函数和复制赋值是显式用户定义的。 析构函数、移动构造函数和移动赋值运算符默认由编译器提供，但这必须发生在 .cpp 文件中，因为标头中 Widget::Impl 是不完整类型，并且 unique_ptr 的删除器需要知道 Impl 的大小。

尽管不再需要手动处理资源，但常量问题以及移动后的空 pimpl_ 对象仍然相同。

使用 std::indirect 实现

这就是 std::indirect 发挥作用的地方。 这是 C++26 中的新词汇类型，在 <memory> 标头中定义。 它旨在用于动态分配但需要表现得像值的类成员。 它被设计用来代替 std::unique_ptr，因为它的语义（不可复制、不传播 const、可以为 null）不合适。 Pimpl 就是一个完美的例子。

以下代码片段显示了使用 std::indirect 实现的 Widget 类：

#pragma once

#include <memory>
#include <string>

class Widget
{
public:
    Widget(const std::string& name);

    Widget(const Widget&);
    Widget(Widget&&) noexcept;
    Widget& operator=(const Widget&);
    Widget& operator=(Widget&&) noexcept;
    ~Widget();

    void click();
    int  clickCount() const;
    std::string label() const;

private:
    struct Impl;
    std::indirect<Impl> pimpl_;
};
#include "widget.h"

struct Widget::Impl
{
    std::string name;
    int         clicks = 0;

    explicit Impl(std::string n) : name(std::move(n)) {}
};

Widget::Widget(const std::string& name)
    : pimpl_(std::in_place, name) 
{}

Widget::Widget(const Widget&) = default;
Widget::Widget(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(const Widget&) = default;
Widget& Widget::operator=(Widget&&) noexcept = default;
Widget::~Widget() = default;

void Widget::click()              { ++pimpl_->clicks; }
int  Widget::clickCount() const   { return pimpl_->clicks; }
std::string Widget::label() const { return pimpl_->name; }

如果我们比较这些实现，我们会看到五个特殊成员函数仍然在标头中声明，但它们在源文件中默认（用于编译器实现），其中 Impl 类型是完整的并且编译器已知（这与 std::unique_ptr 的要求相同）。

std::indirect 类型拥有在堆上分配的单个 T 对象，但其行为类似于 T 成员：

  • 深层复制：复制 std::indirect<T> 对象会复制拥有的对象，因此类的编译器生成的复制构造函数可以正常工作。
  • Const 传播：通过 const std::indirect<T> 对象，您只能获得对 T 的 const 访问。
  • 绝不为 null：它始终保存一个值，除了移出状态（但具有一个名为 valueless_after_move() 的成员函数，可让您检查状态）。

使用 std::indirect 并不妨碍我们显式声明和默认特殊成员函数，但确实帮助我们传播常量，这意味着代码对于意外更改更加稳健。

valueless_after_move() 基本上替代了 unique_ptr 的空检查。 例如，我们可以向所有使用 pimpl_ 对象的函数添加断言，以确保它们在小部件移动后不会被调用。

void Widget::click() 
{
    assert(!pimpl_.valueless_after_move() && "use of moved-from Widget");
    ++pimpl_->clicks;
}

使用 valueless_after_move() 的另一个例子是从我们移出对象的向量中擦除删除。 这是一个例子：

std::vector<std::indirect<Widget>> widgets = ...;
std::vector<std::indirect<Widget>> selected;

for (auto& w : widgets)
    if (select(w))
        selected.push_back(std::move(w));   // leaves a valueless widget behind

std::erase_if(widgets, [](const auto& w) {
    return w.valueless_after_move();        // remove the dangling object
});

这对于 std::unique_ptr 也是可能的，但检查将是 w == nullptr。 因此，这只是检查 std::indirect 对象是否仍保留值的更详细方法。

std::indirect 附带了一个同伴 std::polymorphic，但我们将在另一篇文章中讨论它。

在撰写本文时，仅 GCC 16 支持 std::indirect。

参见

  • PImpl
  • 用于编译时封装的 Pimpl（现代 C++）
  • 现代 C++：Pimpl 习惯用法
  • P3019R14：间接和多态：复合类设计的词汇类型
